label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's think about this question about causation. The question is asking whether the hunter intentionally shot the bird-watcher. The scenario is that a hunter was waiting in the woods to shoot a deer. He sees a large deer that would ensure his victory in a competition if he kills it. While aiming, he notices bird-watchers on the other side of the deer. He realizes the bullet might hit one of them, but he doesn't care and decides to shoot the deer anyway. The bullet does hit one of the bird-watchers. From a philosophical or legal perspective, this is a question about intent and causation. "Intentionally" usually means that the person aimed to achieve a certain outcome. In this case, the hunter's intention was to shoot the deer, not to harm the bird-watchers. Even though he knew there was a risk to the bird-watchers, he didn't intend to harm them. He only wanted to kill the deer to win the competition. From a typical person's perspective, they would likely say "No" to the question of whether the hunter intentionally shot the bird-watcher.
